title: Consider building with PGO for improved performance
body:
Go features profile guided optimisation (PGO) support since a few releases back. By simply generating a profile using pprof and saving it as `default.pgo` in the same folder as the main package, the compiler will automatically apply optimisations. The docs mention that this has been found to generally improve performance by about 2-14% as of 1.22 and I would assume that it might be similar or better as of Go 1.25.

I think it would be worth considering adding a profile for this as the process is easy to apply, it can improve performance for basically all users and it will be applied automatically for anyone building the project.

Reference: https://go.dev/doc/pgo

comments.body:
podman has a `--cpu-profile` flag that can be used to create the profile. But the more important question how would generate this profile to actually reflect the typical workload. There are so many podman commands and it would be hard to find something that works for everything I guess?

The first step would be to write proper benchmarks and compare the performance then with various creating profiles to see if it even makes a difference.

The documentation has some good suggestions on how to merge profiles so you can probably run different workflows and get a single profile. With a benchmark suite good enough, you can of course also output a profile from when running `go test ./...` within the project. But yeah, I agree that writing benchmarks and verifying that some crude initial profiles improve performance is a good start.

Also how does this work when the code changes? Per https://go.dev/doc/pgo#source-stability it sounds like while basic compatibility should be there you still would have to generate the profiles quite often for it to work properly long term.
And at this point it sounds like a major maintenance burden to keep this updated.

Podman is a project with a ton of different users that use it all in different ways, distributions patch the source and/or use different go version to build so I am not sure how much value we would get out of these profiles anyway.

> Also how does this work when the code changes?

It will only be able to apply performance optimizations to parts of the code that it knows about from the profile. If the code changes, it might not be able to optimize it and if new code is added it obviously can't optimize that either but I think that is a fine trade off. As I understand it, it is written in a way so that a bad profile never will decrease performance basically. If the profile works then you get better performance.

I'd say that it is fine to regenerate the profiles for each minor release but keep them the same for patch releases. The Go compiler project themselves only generate a new profile at the end of the release cycle for each 1.X release and they still find it useful enough.

> And at this point it sounds like a major maintenance burden to keep this updated.

Assuming that benchmarks show an improvement and it is done at the end of each release cycle (that isn't a patch release), I don't think it should be a problem. The performance improvement likely outweighs the time spent generating a profile and especially if the profile can be generated and verified in a ln automated consistent manner.

> Podman is a project with a ton of different users that use it all in different ways, distributions patch the source and/or use different go version to build so I am not sure how much value we would get out of these profiles anyway.

Per my answer above, I don't think that should be much of an issue. Older Go versions than what you generated the profiles from will still compile fine. If it can't apply optimizations it just can't apply them. The profiles are also independent of OS/ARCH (unless you have specific code protected behind go:build directives) so it should still apply no matter where it was generated vs where you apply it.

> I'd say that it is fine to regenerate the profiles for each minor release but keep them the same for patch releases. The Go compiler project themselves only generate a new profile at the end of the release cycle for each 1.X release and they still find it useful enough.

Sure, still work that someone must do. To be clear I am not against it per se, but I like to see some actual benchmarks that this actually makes a meaningful difference. And if someone else (e.g. you) likes to maintain the profile generation then even better.
Absolutely. I definitely agree with that. Given how many users use Podman, I think there is a nice opportunity to try and see if we can improve the performance for a lot of people if PGO works out :)
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
